daniel_zhu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniel Zhu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Daniel is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Daniel has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Daniel saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniel has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniel showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Daniel traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SoCal CRC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Daniel entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Daniel paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Daniel is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Daniel wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Daniel, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

daniil_filimonov.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daniil Filimonov, from Michigan, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Daniil is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Daniil has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Daniil saga began at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daniil plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Daniil has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Md. Amaan Ali Pasha has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daniil has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daniil showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Daniil traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Daniil entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Daniil paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Daniil keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Daniil has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Daniil, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Daniil. We will be here, taking notes.

danny_luo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Danny Luo, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Danny has 29 career entries across 12 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Danny saga began at 2023 SoCal CRC (SGVBC) with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Danny has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Danny's texts. The longest-running partnership is with William Lin: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope William Lin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Owen Wang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Danny has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Danny has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Danny has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Danny entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Danny paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Danny keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Danny has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Danny, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Danny. We will be here, taking notes.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Daphne Huang, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 21 career entries in 8 tournaments, Daphne has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Daphne drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Daphne saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Daphne plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Daphne has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Stella Pan: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Stella Pan has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U17 — we hope Stella Pan has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Daphne entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Daphne's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Daphne and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Daphne has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Daphne is not just losing locally — Daphne is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Daphne can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Daphne has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Daphne showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Daphne entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Daphne paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Daphne keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Daphne has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Daphne, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Daphne. We will be here, taking notes.

darmikh_pallabothula.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Darmikh Pallabothula, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 24 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 24-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Darmikh's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Darmikh keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Darmikh saga began at 2023 South ORC with 1 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Darmikh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Darmikh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Darmikh's texts. The go-to partner is Saipranav Kalla (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Saipranav Kalla has emotionally recovered.

Darmikh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darmikh has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darmikh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Darmikh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 South ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Darmikh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Darmikh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 24 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Darmikh. Darmikh just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Darmikh has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Darmikh. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Darmikh: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Darren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Darren entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Darren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 25 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Darren. Darren just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Darren has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Darren. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Darren: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

darren_tse.name: Darren Tse
darren_tse.summary:
A Washington competitor, Darren Tse has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 13 entries across 5 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

What makes Darren especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Darren has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Sanjay Chittella (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Amber Chang — 2 events in XD, best: #2
  • Daniel Bicheng Fong — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

Darren has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Darren's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
darren_tse.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Darren Tse, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 1 win across 13 entries in 5 tournaments. That is a 8% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Darren shows up to a tournament, Darren pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Darren saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2024 SBC NW CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Darren's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 8 entries — a stretch where Darren actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Darren is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Darren experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Darren has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SBC NW CRC, where Darren entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Darren does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Darren has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Darren is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Darren plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Darren is 0-for-5. Zero titles in 5 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Darren and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Darren needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Darren's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Darren has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Darren has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Darren showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Darren traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Darren entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Darren paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 13 entries, and 12 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Darren looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Darren's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Darren has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Darren. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

darron_jaden_su.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Darron Jaden Su, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 25 career entries in 9 tournaments, Darron has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 24 entries where Darron drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 25 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Darron saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Darron plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Darron has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Darron's texts. The go-to partner is Tanvi Deshmukh (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Dhruva Singh has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Darron entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Darron's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Darron and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Darron has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Darron has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Darron has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Darron entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Darron paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Darron keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Darron has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Darron, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Darron. We will be here, taking notes.

The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Darsh entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Darsh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Darsh keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Darsh has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Darsh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Darsh. We will be here, taking notes.

The Darshan saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Darshan is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Darshan's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Darshan is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

david_purnomo.name: David Purnomo
david_purnomo.summary:
Representing Washington, David Purnomo has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and David's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, David has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

David is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

David has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Daniel Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Kavya Krishna (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

David has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

David has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, David brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
david_purnomo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for David Purnomo, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 35 career entries in 13 tournaments, David has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 34 entries where David drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 35 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The David saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where David burst onto the scene with a title in BD U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, David has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what David promised and what David has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 18 entries — a stretch where David actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and David is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the David experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. David has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 NW CRC, where David entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, David does own 1 actual title, so it is not like David has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means David is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. David plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — David is 0-for-9. Zero titles in 9 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside David and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: David needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. David has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers David's texts. The go-to partner is Thomas Wang (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Krisha Tyagi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of David's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — David entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at David's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at David and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, David has shown up 3 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

David has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because David is not just losing locally — David is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Purnomo family travels cross-country so David can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. David has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where David showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means David traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where David entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying David paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 35 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — David looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on David's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and David has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, David. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

david_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for David Zhang, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. David has 50 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The David saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, David would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times David has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times David has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in David's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. David plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 50 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. David has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers David's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Derek Huang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Derek Huang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U11 — we hope Venba Senkadir has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, David has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because David is not just losing locally — David is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so David can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. David has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where David showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means David traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where David entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying David paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 50 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not David. David just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and David has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like David. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, David: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Deeksha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Deeksha is 0-for-2. Zero titles in 2 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Deeksha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Deeksha needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo.

Deeksha is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Deeksha wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Deeksha, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

deekshith_vasanth.name: Deekshith Vasanth

Deekshith competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Deekshith brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
deekshith_vasanth.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Deekshith Vasanth, from Oregon, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Deekshith's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Deekshith keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Deekshith saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Deekshith has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.'

Deekshith has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Deekshith has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Deekshith showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC in BS U13, which means Deekshith traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 NW OLC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Bellevue NW ORC, where Deekshith entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Deekshith paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 12 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Deekshith. Deekshith just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Deekshith has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Deekshith. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Deekshith: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

deepika_vaishnavi_venkatraman.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Deepika Vaishnavi Venkatraman, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Deepika has 37 career entries across 14 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Deepika saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Deepika would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Deepika has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Deepika has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Deepika's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Deepika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Deepika has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Deepika's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Andy Starostin: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Andy Starostin has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Andy Starostin has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Deepika has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Deepika has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Deepika is not just losing locally — Deepika is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Venkatraman family travels cross-country so Deepika can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Deepika has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Deepika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U13, which means Deepika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Deepika entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Deepika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Deepika keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Deepika has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Deepika, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Deepika. We will be here, taking notes.

dennis_kwok.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dennis Kwok, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Dennis is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Dennis has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Dennis saga began at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Dennis is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Dennis's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Dennis is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dennis has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dennis showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in BS U13, which means Dennis traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Dennis is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Dennis wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Dennis, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

dennis_shao.name: Dennis Shao
dennis_shao.summary:
Hailing from Washington, Dennis Shao is a tenacious competitor with 3 quarterfinal-or-better finishes, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 20 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Dennis competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Dennis has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Nicholas Volfbeyn — a trusted BD partner (12 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Dennis has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dennis has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Dennis brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dennis_shao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dennis Shao, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Dennis has 43 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Dennis saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dennis plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 43 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Dennis has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dennis's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Nicholas Volfbeyn: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U17 — we hope Nicholas Volfbeyn has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Dennis has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dennis is not just losing locally — Dennis is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shao family travels cross-country so Dennis can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dennis has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dennis showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Dennis traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Dennis entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Dennis paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 43 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Dennis. Dennis just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Dennis has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Dennis. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Dennis: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

derek_huang.name: Derek Huang
derek_huang.summary:
Representing Washington, Derek Huang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Derek's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Derek is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Derek has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Seanna Yang (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (15 total events in XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • David Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (5 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Leslie Wang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #5)

Derek has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Derek has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Derek consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 4 titles, Derek has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
derek_huang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Derek Huang, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Derek has 59 career entries across 22 tournaments and has managed to win 4 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Derek saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Derek's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Derek experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Derek has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 SBC NW CRC, where Derek entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Derek does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Derek has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 4 wins means Derek is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Derek can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The go-to partner is Seanna Yang (15 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BD U13 — we hope Iniyan Arun has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Derek's 4 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Derek has shown up 9 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Derek a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Derek has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 OBA NW OLC in BS U11, where Derek was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Derek is not just losing locally — Derek is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Derek can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Derek entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 59 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Derek looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Derek's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Derek has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Derek. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
