The Li family travels cross-country so Derek can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 14 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Derek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Derek keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Derek has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Derek, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Derek. We will be here, taking notes.

derek_royce_chan.name: Derek Royce Chan
derek_royce_chan.summary:
Representing Washington, Derek Royce Chan has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 25 entries across 9 tournaments, Derek is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

Derek is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Derek adapts to anyone:
  • Nirmalya Routray (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Derek has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, NW, Derek isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Derek brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
derek_royce_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Derek Royce Chan, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Derek has 25 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Derek saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Rheya Huang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Derek has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Derek has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Derek entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 25 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Derek. Derek just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Derek has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Derek. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Derek: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

derek_ruoyu_li.name: Derek Ruoyu Li
derek_ruoyu_li.summary:
A New Jersey competitor, Derek Ruoyu Li knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U19 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 28 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Derek competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Derek adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)

Derek has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Derek is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
derek_ruoyu_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Derek Ruoyu Li, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 win across 28 entries in 13 tournaments. That is a 4% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Derek shows up to a tournament, Derek pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Derek saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Derek's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 11 entries — a stretch where Derek actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Derek is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Derek experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Derek has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Derek does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Derek has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Derek is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Derek plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Derek is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Derek and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Derek needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Derek has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Derek's texts. The go-to partner is Ethan Chen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Will Luo has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Derek's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Derek has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Derek has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Derek showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Derek traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Derek entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Derek paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 28 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Derek looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Derek's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Derek has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Derek. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

devaansh_mahato.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Devaansh Mahato, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Devaansh has 36 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 6% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Devaansh saga began at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Devaansh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 22 entries — a stretch where Devaansh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Devaansh is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Devaansh experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Devaansh has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC, where Devaansh entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Devaansh does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Devaansh has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 2 wins means Devaansh is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devaansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devaansh is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devaansh and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devaansh needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Devaansh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Devaansh's texts. The go-to partner is Venba Senkadir (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U11 — we hope Hector Jingbo Wang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Devaansh's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Devaansh has shown up 5 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devaansh is not just losing locally — Devaansh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mahato family travels cross-country so Devaansh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devaansh has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devaansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Devaansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Devaansh entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Devaansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 36 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Devaansh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Devaansh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Devaansh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Devaansh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

devaansh_surpur.name: Devaansh Surpur
devaansh_surpur.summary:

Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Devaansh's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Devaansh competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Devaansh adapts to anyone:

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 7 tournaments — a clear sign that Devaansh is still ascending.

Devaansh has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Devaansh's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.

devangi_kohli.name: Devangi Kohli
devangi_kohli.summary:
A Illinois competitor, Devangi Kohli has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 4 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

Devangi competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Devangi has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Devangi brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
devangi_kohli.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Devangi Kohli, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 4 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Devangi is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Devangi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Devangi saga began at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devangi has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devangi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Devangi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Devangi entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Devangi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Devangi keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Devangi has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Devangi, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Devangi. We will be here, taking notes.

The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devin is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Devin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Devin's texts. The go-to partner is Ella Mak (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Rubie Liu has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devin is not just losing locally — Devin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Garg family travels cross-country so Devin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devin has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17, which means Devin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NW ORC, where Devin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Devin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 29 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Devin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Devin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Devin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Devin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

devin_shao.name: Devin Shao
devin_shao.summary:
A Washington competitor, Devin Shao has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Devin's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Devin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Devin has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Ethan Biren Fong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (13 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang — a trusted XD partner (5 events, best: #4)
  • Daniel Seungchan Park — 2 events, 1 title together in BD

A veteran of the junior circuit, Devin has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Devin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Devin has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
devin_shao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Devin Shao, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Devin has 46 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Devin saga began at 2021 NW CRC, where Devin burst onto the scene with a title in BD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Devin has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Devin promised and what Devin has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 9 entries — a stretch where Devin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Devin is 0-for-2. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Devin would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Devin has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Devin has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Devin's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Devin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Devin is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Devin and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Devin needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Devin has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ethan Biren Fong: 13 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 13 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ethan Biren Fong has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Selina Lei has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Devin's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Devin has shown up 5 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Devin is not just losing locally — Devin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shao family travels cross-country so Devin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Devin has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Devin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15, which means Devin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Devin entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Devin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 46 entries, and 45 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Devin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Devin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Devin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Devin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

dhev_sudheer.name: Dhev Sudheer
dhev_sudheer.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Dhev Sudheer is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Dhev is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Dhev adapts to anyone:
  • Isha Haldankar (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Harshith Yoganand (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Dhev has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dhev has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Dhev brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dhev_sudheer.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dhev Sudheer, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 29 events across 11 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 29-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Dhev's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Dhev keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Dhev saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Dhev would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Dhev has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Dhev has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Dhev's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dhev plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Dhev has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dhev's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Varad V Vaishnav has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dhev is not just losing locally — Dhev is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sudheer family travels cross-country so Dhev can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhev has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhev showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13, which means Dhev traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Dhev entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Dhev paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Dhev. Dhev just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Dhev has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Dhev. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Dhev: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

dhiraj_praveen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dhiraj Praveen, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Dhiraj has 1 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Dhiraj saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 1 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Dhiraj is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Dhiraj's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Dhiraj is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

At Junior Nationals, Dhiraj has shown up 1 time with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Dhiraj is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Dhiraj wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Dhiraj, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Dhruva is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Dhruva's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Dhruva is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhruva has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhruva showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Dhruva traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Dhruva is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Dhruva wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Dhruva, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

dhruva_singh.name: Dhruva Singh
dhruva_singh.summary:
A California competitor, Dhruva Singh keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 4 times, currently competing at the U19 level, building a strong foundation across 12 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Dhruva competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Dhruva has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Sohum Gulla Pulijal (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Ardra Mohan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Dhruva has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Dhruva has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Dhruva brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dhruva_singh.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dhruva Singh, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 27 career entries in 12 tournaments, Dhruva has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 26 entries where Dhruva drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 27 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Dhruva saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dhruva plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Dhruva has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dhruva's texts. The go-to partner is Sohum Gulla Pulijal (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Darron Jaden Su has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Dhruva entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Dhruva's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Dhruva and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Dhruva has shown up 2 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dhruva is not just losing locally — Dhruva is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Singh family travels cross-country so Dhruva can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhruva has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhruva showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19, which means Dhruva traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Dhruva entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Dhruva paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Dhruva keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Dhruva has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Dhruva, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Dhruva. We will be here, taking notes.

Dhruvan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Dhruvan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Dhruvan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Dhruvan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

dhvani_shankar.name: Dhvani Shankar
dhvani_shankar.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Dhvani Shankar has climbed to the top of the mountain at Junior Nationals, the crown jewel of the American junior badminton season in GD U11 - Position 5-8. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 4 career victories across 22 tournaments, Dhvani has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Dhvani is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Dhvani competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (4 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (GD U11 - Position 5-8).

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Dhvani adapts to anyone:
  • Meghana Pandiri (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (8 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Benson Liang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Advik Kevlani — 6 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 3 seasons

Having moved up to U17, Dhvani is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Dhvani brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Dhvani has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Dhvani has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Dhvani consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 4 titles, Dhvani has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
dhvani_shankar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dhvani Shankar, from Georgia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Dhvani IS a Junior National Champion — GD U11 - Position 5-8 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 57-entry career across 22 tournaments with a 7% win rate, which means 53 times — 53! — Dhvani went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 57 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Dhvani saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Dhvani burst onto the scene with a title in GD U11 - Position 5-8 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Dhvani has delivered on some of those promises: 4 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Dhvani promised and what Dhvani has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 2 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Dhvani actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Dhvani is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Dhvani experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Dhvani has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 South Houston CRC, where Dhvani entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Dhvani does own 4 actual titles, so it is not like Dhvani has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 4 wins means Dhvani is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dhvani plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Dhvani can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Dhvani has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dhvani's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Meghana Pandiri: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Meghana Pandiri has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Advik Kevlani has emotionally recovered.

Of Dhvani's 4 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Dhvani feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Dhvani entered 4 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Dhvani's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Dhvani and said 'nah.' Tournament committees have handed Dhvani a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Dhvani has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 MBC Midwest OLC in GS U15, where Dhvani was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Dhvani of is playing it safe. Dhvani has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15, U17 — which means Dhvani has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 2 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Dhvani has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dhvani is not just losing locally — Dhvani is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shankar family travels cross-country so Dhvani can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dhvani has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dhvani showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in XD U15, which means Dhvani traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Dhvani entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Dhvani paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 4 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 53 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Dhvani looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Dhvani's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Dhvani has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Dhvani. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

diksha_chandrasekaran.name: Diksha Chandrasekaran
diksha_chandrasekaran.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Diksha Chandrasekaran has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Diksha's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Diksha has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Diksha especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 doubles title, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Diksha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Mark w Wu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (5 events, best: #5)
  • Sophia Sun (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U15 (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Diksha has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Diksha has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 5 titles, Diksha is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
diksha_chandrasekaran.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Diksha Chandrasekaran, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 80 career entries in 28 tournaments, Diksha has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 15 entries where Diksha drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 80 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Diksha saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13) with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2024 DFW South OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Diksha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Diksha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Diksha is 0-for-16. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diksha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diksha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Diksha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diksha's texts. The go-to partner is Yifei Zhang (21 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GD U15 — we hope Jessica Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Diksha's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diksha entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Diksha's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diksha and said 'nah.'

Diksha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diksha is not just losing locally — Diksha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chandrasekaran family travels cross-country so Diksha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diksha has 25 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diksha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Diksha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Diksha entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Diksha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 80 entries, and 75 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Diksha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Diksha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Diksha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Diksha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Disha and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Disha needs help. Now, about the quality of those wins. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Disha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.
