diya_rajan.name: Diya Rajan
diya_rajan.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Diya Rajan is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U19 level, with 17 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Diya especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Diya has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Aditya Prasad Iyer (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (6 events, spanning 3 seasons)

Diya has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Diya brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
diya_rajan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Diya Rajan, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 17 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 17-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Diya's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Diya keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Diya saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. The longest-running partnership is with Aditya Prasad Iyer: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Aditya Prasad Iyer has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Aditya Prasad Iyer has emotionally recovered.

Diya has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diya has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17, which means Diya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Diya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Diya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Diya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Diya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Diya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Diya. We will be here, taking notes.

diya_shetty.name: Diya Shetty
diya_shetty.summary:
Representing Virginia, Diya Shetty is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Diya in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Diya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Diya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 4 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U15 GD) • Junior Nationals top-3 (GD U15, GS U15) • 10 ORC titles.

Diya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Sanjanaa Shyam (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)
  • Aadya Pitani (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (3 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Diya has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Diya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Diya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 11 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 11 titles, Diya is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
diya_shetty.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Diya Shetty, from Virginia, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 61 career entries in 23 tournaments, Diya has 11 titles — a 18% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 4 entries where Diya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 61 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Diya saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U11-U13), where Diya burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Diya has delivered on some of those promises: 11 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Diya promised and what Diya has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 6 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Diya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Diya is 0-for-11. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Diya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Diya has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Diya entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U13, GD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Diya does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Diya has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 11 wins means Diya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Diya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 4 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Diya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diya's texts. The go-to partner is Vincent Chen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Darsh Jain has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diya entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Diya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diya and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Diya 6 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Diya has been seeded in 16 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Diya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, and finishing 65th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Diya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Diya of is playing it safe. Diya has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Diya has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 5 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Diya has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diya is not just losing locally — Diya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 18% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Shetty family travels cross-country so Diya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Diya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Diya entered 3 events with results ranging from 1st to 65th. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Diya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 11 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Diya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Diya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Diya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Diya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Diyaa can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Diyaa has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Diyaa's texts. The go-to partner is Eshan Neog (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Sai Sahasra Gajula [] has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Diyaa's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Diyaa entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Diyaa's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Diyaa and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Diyaa has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Diyaa has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Diyaa is not just losing locally — Diyaa is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sarath family travels cross-country so Diyaa can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Diyaa has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Diyaa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Diyaa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Diyaa entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Diyaa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 40 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Diyaa looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Diyaa's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Diyaa has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Diyaa. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

don_henley_averia.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Don HENLEY Averia, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Don has 3 career entries across 1 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Don saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Don experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Don has reached the finals 2 times — at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Don has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Don plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 3 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Don has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell.

Don is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Don wants to be. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Dwijesh has 61 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 5 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Dwijesh saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 NE MassBad OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Dwijesh's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Dwijesh actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Dwijesh is 0-for-12. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dwijesh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Dwijesh can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Dwijesh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Dwijesh's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Adithya S Burisetty: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Adithya S Burisetty has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Disha Vijayakumar has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Dwijesh's 5 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Dwijesh has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Dwijesh a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Dwijesh has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 NE NJBC CRC in BS U17, where Dwijesh was seeded #1 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Dwijesh has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Dwijesh is not just losing locally — Dwijesh is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Makireddi family travels cross-country so Dwijesh can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dwijesh has 27 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dwijesh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Dwijesh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Dwijesh entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Dwijesh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 61 entries, and 56 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Dwijesh looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Dwijesh's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Dwijesh has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Dwijesh. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

dylan_ji_daniels.name: Dylan Ji Daniels
dylan_ji_daniels.summary:
Out of California, Dylan Ji Daniels keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 3 times, currently competing at the U17 level, building a strong foundation across 8 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

A true triple-threat, Dylan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Dylan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Dylan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
dylan_ji_daniels.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Dylan Ji Daniels, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 8 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Dylan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Dylan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Dylan saga began at 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Dylan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Dylan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Tsz Wai Lam has emotionally recovered.

Dylan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Dylan has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Dylan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U17, which means Dylan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Dylan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Dylan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 14 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Dylan. Dylan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Dylan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Dylan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Dylan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
